summary: Julia Evans describes her Hacker School project of writing a minimal TCP stack in Python using the scapy library, with the goal of fetching a webpage from Google. She walks through the TCP handshake, discovers that her kernel's existing TCP stack interferes by sending RST packets, and solves this with ARP spoofing to use a fake IP address. After getting it working, she finds that Python is too slow to send ACKs fast enough, causing Google's server to reset the connection. Despite only working 25% of the time, she considers the project more fun and instructive than writing a proper TCP stack in C.
key_points: Writing a TCP stack in userspace conflicts with the kernel's own TCP stack, which sends RST packets for connections it didn't initiate
ARP spoofing to claim a different IP address is a clever hack to bypass the kernel's interference with raw socket TCP
Python's performance is too slow for the millisecond-level timing TCP expects for ACK responses, causing servers to assume connection problems
The TCP spec is extremely complicated, but servers will respond to you even if your implementation is extremely sketchy
Building a broken version of something from scratch can be more instructive than building a correct version in the right language
money_quotes: I didn't care much about proper error handling or anything; I just wanted to get one webpage and declare victory :)
my kernel: lol wtf I never asked for this! RST!
This is when I remembered that I *already* have a TCP stack on my computer, in my kernel.
Google was sending me packets way faster than my program could keep up and send ACKs.
If you're actually writing a production TCP stack, don't use Python.
The TCP spec is really complicated, but you can get servers to reply to you even if your implementation is extremely sketchy.
I think this was actually way more fun and instructive than trying to write a TCP stack in an appropriate language like C :)
key_insight: Building a deliberately impractical TCP stack in Python reveals how networking actually works — from kernel interference to timing constraints — in ways that reading specs or using proper tools never would.
